editionStmt.edition: Digital Critical Edition of the Chapter Summaries (Capitula) to Ruth in the Tours Bibles

publicationStmt.p:
Welcome to the digital critical edition project of the Capitula to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles!
The editions are part of the PRIN 2022 PNRR project DOBiPS – Data Oriented Biblical Paratext Studies, awarded for the 2023–2025 period to the research units at the Universities of Udine (P.I. E. Colombi) and Cassino (P.I. R. Casavecchia), under the competitive PRIN PNRR call – National Recovery and Resilience Plan, Mission 4 "Education and Research", funded by the European Union’s NextGenerationEU programme (protocol no. P2022ZW4AW).
The Capitula prefixed to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles have been encoded in XML/TEI and published using the software EVT (Edition Visualization Technology).
The editions were prepared by members of the Udine research unit: Emanuela Colombi, Margherita Filippozzi, and Silverio Franzoni.
Details of the encoding choices and related methodological reflections have been presented in the working paper: E. Colombi, M. Filippozzi, S. Franzoni, The Digital Critical Edition of the Capitula to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles, DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.16761173.
Information on the individual editions is available by clicking the 'info' button at the top of the left-hand pane.

bibl.author: Donatien de Bruyne
bibl.title: Sommaires, divisions et rubriques de la Bible latine

bibl.author: Bernhard Bischoff
bibl.title: Katalog der festländischen Handschriften des neunten Jahrhunderts (mit Ausnahme der wisigotischen)

source.p: First period of the Turonian production according to Fischer's classification (p. 257).

II + 214 folios

handDesc.handNote: Carolingian minuscule by different hands (at least five)

source.p: Excluded from the Turonian production according to Fischer's classification (p. 252).
Second period of the Turonian production according to Fischer's classification (p. 259).

msDesc.head: Biblia latina Veteris et Novi Testamenti

source.p: Third period of the Turonian production under Fridugisus (periode II) according to Fischer's classification (p. 261).

source.p: Fourth period of the Turonian production under Fridugisus (periode II) according to Fischer's classification (p. 262).

msDesc.head: Biblia latina Veteris et Novi Testamenti

source.p: Second period of the Turonian production under Adelard (periode III) according to Fischer's classification (p. 264).

msDesc.head: Biblia latina ("First Bible of Charles the Bald")

supportDesc.extent:
I + 423 folios
height 495 mm width 375 mm

source.p: Fourth period (Abbot Vivian, 843-851) of the Turonian production according to Fischer's classification (p. 267).

msDesc.head: Biblia latina Veteris et Novi Testamenti (from Ex 16:28 to Apc 6:9)

supportDesc.extent:
III + 176 + III folios
height 490 mm width 365 mm

source.p: Fifth period (Abbot Vivian, 853-c.900) of the Turonian production according to Fischer's classification (p. 269).

listWit.witness: text of Tur series as printed by D. de Bruyne, Sommaires..., p. 49

tagsDecl.rendition: the digital reproduction is b/w, impossible to determine color

front.p:
This digital edition presents the reconstruction of the capitula of series Tur (according to Donatien de Bruyne's classification) that precede the Book of Ruth, in the textual form known from the Tours scriptorium (Φ).
This series is the only one attested in the Tours Bibles and, insofar as the data gathered within the DOBiPS project allow to judge, it is also the most widespread of the three series known to de Bruyne (p. 49). However, the presence of a set of capitula for the book of Ruth is far from consistent: only about 20% of the manuscripts recorded in our database contain one; moreover, its occurrence decreases over time, since the vast majority of the manuscripts that present one date from the 9th century. Note that the Maurdramnus Bible does not present any table of chapters for the book of Ruth (which is contained in its second volume, now Amiens, Bibliothèque Municipale, 7).
The text of Tur is likely based on a Vulgate version of the book of Ruth, but the limited degree of literal overlap between the individual capitula and the biblical text makes certainty difficult to reach. The most substantial correspondences are found in capitula 1 and 10:
cap. 1 (Rt 1:1) facta fame in terra iudae Tur : facta est fames in terra Vulg.
ibid. (ibid.) elimelech de bethleem iuda abiit cum uxore sua noemi et duobus filiis in terra
moabitide Tur : abiitque homo de Bethleem Iuda ut peregrinaretur in regione moabitide cum uxore
sua ac duobus liberis Vulg. (for filiis vs liberis see also the note ad loc.)
cap. 10 (Rt 4:17) uocatum est nomen filii obeth hic est pater isai patris dauid Tur : uocauerunt
nomen eius Obed hic est pater Isai patris Dauid Vulg. It should be noted, however, that no pre-Hieronymian text of Ruth survives in a complete and continuous form that would allow for a systematic comparison with Tur.
Within this series, each capitulum summarizes a portion of the biblical text of varying length, presenting its content in a fairly complete and factual manner. As a result, Tur provides a good summary of the book of Ruth.
With the exception of the last, in Z (Zürich, Zentralbibl., Car C 1) each capitulum is copied only up to the point where the line ends, whereas the remainder is omitted regardless of sense or syntactic coherence. This shows that, already at an early date, the capitula could be regarded as an unnecessary complement to the biblical text, irrespective of their actual usefulness. Moreover, this also proves that, at least with regard to the Tur series, none of the other manuscripts can descend from Z.
From a philological perspective, this is essentially all that can be said about the transmission of this series: our witnesses do not exhibit any further distinctive feature that would allow a convincing establishment of groupings or relationships among them.
Throughout the edition, certain words are highlighted in different ways in order to signal noteworthy features. For technical reasons, the filter tool ("Filtri" button below the text) cannot be customized by the reader, but it is permanently active in order to display certain remarkable features. In the edited text (view mode "Testo di lettura"), colors indicate the reason (the @cause attribute) for specific readings, as follows:
grammatical: pink;
orthographic: blue. In each witness's text (view mode "Collazione"), the filters highlight (in brown) the points where a correction was made.
Furthermore, in the edited text readings that diverge from de Bruyne's edition are highlighted in bold type.
Occasional commentary on the readings is provided within the text itself (clickable red dots); further remarks can be found under the apparatus (right-hand panel of the screen "Testo di lettura"), either by clicking on "Nota critica" or by accessing the XML structure (when the information refers to a single witness and is therefore embedded in a witDetail element).
The point in the text of Ruth where each witness places the chapter number corresponding to each capitulum can be accessed either by clicking on the two-page icon next to each capitulum number, or by clicking on the "Passi paralleli" thumbnail (in the right-hand panel of the "Testo di lettura" screen).
In Tu, these chapter numbers are often no longer visible because of faded ink.

item.title:
Facta acta
est The insertion of est may be due to the influence of the corresponding biblical passage (Rt 1:1), facta est fames in terra. Note, however, that the related noun fame was not adapted to the nominative.
fame fama famae in terra iudae iudȩ iudeae iudae iudeae
elimelech helimelech de bethleem bethlem
iuda iudae iuda
abiit cum uxore sua noemi
et
duobus
duabus
filiis
filiis The Vulgate (Rt 1:1) reads duobus liberis, whereas filiis is found in some pre-Hieronymian versions and patristic citations. However, given the proximity of the two terms, it is probably unnecessary to suppose the influence of any external source on the text of this capitulum.
in terra
moabitide moabitidae
causa
uictualium uictulium
quo mortuo quortuo quo mortuo
filii fili
eius accipientes uxores et ipsi mortui sunt

Noemi oemi reuertentem ad patriam ruth nurus
deserere deferre noluit
ad patriam suam
quae quȩ quæ
perueniens perueneens perueniens
in bethleem iuda
perrexit perrexerit
in agrum
booz booth
ad
colligendas
colligendum colligendas
spicas

Booz ooz booth dat licentiam ruth ut de agro eius colligat spicas et
hora
ora
uescendi cum messoribus comedat
pro
pr? pro
eo quod audierat eam bene egisse circa socrum
suam suum

Inquirenti nquirenti noemi indicat iudicat indicat iudicat iudicare
?????
ruth quale in agro booz fecerit opus

Commonita ommonita commita commonita comonita ruth a ad a ad ad a
socru sochru socro socru sua iungitur iungit iungetur puellis booz iterum ad operandum

Reuersa euersa ruth denuo ad socrum suam monetur mouetur
ab ad ea quemadmodum debeat booz
coniugio coniugi coniugio

Ruth uth
instructa intructa instructa infrustra
collocauit conlocauit conlouauit se nocte ad pedes booz qui quia

ab
eius coitu abstinuit

Booz ooz booth boozh
praesentibus presentibus
decem decim X uiris in porta bethleem consuluit propinquiorem se de
nuptiis nptiis nuptiis

Soluit oluit noluit
propinquus propinqus secundum morem calciamentum suum et dedit
booz booth
cessitque
iure uire

booz boozh
ruth accepit uxorem natusque
est
illis
illi According to de Bruyne's edition, illi should be the reading of the Bible of San Paolo fuori le Mura; however, the Benedictine edition, although using this manuscrit, does not acknowledge that reading. Φ, Tu vs de Bruyne (adiaph.) est
filius

bibl.title: Ruth 4:17 natus est filius Noemi (Mb2: 4:17 vicinae autem mulieres)

item.title:
Vocatum ocatum uocatumque est nomen filii obeth filii obeth fili obeth filio boeth filio oboeth filii obed filiobooz filioboech filio obed filio boeh filiobeth filio obed filiobed Since the form of this name is transmitted with considerable variation in our witnesses, the form preferred by both de Bruyne and the Benedictine edition, Obeth, has been retained. In the Weber-Gryson edition of the Vulgate (Rt 4:17), the name appears as Obed.
hic huc hoc id est pater patris isai patris dauid deinde de
sp. vac. rel. Bm
sequuntur secuntur tur sp. vac. ante -tur rel. Bm
generationes a
phares usque pharisȩusque
ad dauid aduid
